name: Ian Dury
description:
'''Ian Robins Dury''' (12 May 1942{{snd}} 27 March 2000) was an English singer, songwriter and actor best remembered as the frontman of [[The Blockheads|Ian Dury and the Blockheads]]. Described by ''[[The Guardian]]'' as "one of few true originals of the English music scene", Dury drew from [[music hall]] and [[punk rock|punk]] traditions, often incorporating observational humour and [[word play]] in his lyrics. 

Dury initially performed with the [[pub rock  (United Kingdom)|pub rock]] band [[Kilburn and the High Roads]] before signing to the [[new wave music|new wave]]-oriented [[Independent record label|independent record label]] [[Stiff Records|Stiff]] in 1977. With the Blockheads, he scored several [[UK singles chart]] hits in the late 1970s, among them "[[What a Waste]]", "[[Reasons to Be Cheerful, Part 3]]" and the chart-topper "[[Hit Me with Your Rhythm Stick]]". In the 1980s, Dury initiated an acting career in film and television. His best-known later recordings include "Profoundly in Love with Pandora", written for the 1985 [[ITV (TV network)|ITV]] series ''[[The Secret Diary of Adrian Mole (TV series)|The Secret Diary of Adrian Mole, Aged 13¾]]'', and the 1981 single "[[Spasticus Autisticus]]". The latter, a [[protest song]] reflecting Dury's life with [[polio]] and his opposition to contemporary attitudes about disability, was performed during the opening of the [[2012 Summer Paralympics|London 2012 Paralympics]].
